coredata.dc:title: Acoustofluidics-manipulated triple-emission fluorescent nanoprobe aggregates with multicolor-variation for colorimetric quantitative assay

Du, Shuhu
coredata.dc:description: Acoustofluidics-assisted colorimetric method, a promising point-of-care test (POCT) strategy, has been applied in convenient detection of target molecules using the sensing nanomaterials, but remains a formidable challenge to design fluorescent probe with profuse color evolution, realizing sensitive, simple and accurate determination of analyte. Here, a novel triple-emission fluorescent probe is constructed through a simple blend of two ratiometric fluorescent probes (blue-organosilane-polymerized carbon dots@SiO2 nanoparticles@red-CdTe/CdS quantum dots (b@SiO2@r) and blue-organosilane-polymerized carbon dots@SiO2 nanoparticles@green-CdTe/CdS quantum dots (b@SiO2@g)) at the optimized volume ratio of 11:8. As signal reporters of the triple-emission probe, outer modified “r” and “g” will be quenched in sequence by target via two-step response strategy while the inner “b” as internal standard remains constant. When acoustic field is applied, the effect of acoustofluidics-based nanoparticles concentration makes triple-emission probe aggregate completely, accompanying fluorescence color signal being enhanced, sensitivity of colorimetric assay being improved and multicolor-variation (from orange to dark-goldenrod to dark-olive-green to sea-green to dark-cyan to final steel-blue) with the increase of analyte amount. For POCT demonstration, hemoglobin (Hb) is used as a model target and the captured fluorescence photos of triple-emission probe aggregates are analyzed by a chromaticity analysis application on the smartphone to calculate the red, green and blue values, which are used for accurate Hb quantification with the limit of detection of 1.99 mg/L. Whole test is finished within ∼12 min. The acoustofluidics-manipulated triple-emission probe biosensing strategy has also been applied to the rapid and visual quantitative detection of Hb in human blood.

(b) HDD and (c) zeta potential of b@SiO2, b@SiO2-PDDA, b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g, respectively. (d) FT-IR spectra of b-SiCDs, r-QDs, b@SiO2 and b@SiO2@r. (g) A typical TEM image of b@SiO2@r (top left) and EDS elemental (C, N, O, Si, Te, Cd, S) mapping images of b@SiO2@r. (h) Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 365 nm) of (1) b@SiO2@r, (2) b@SiO2@g and (3) triple-emission probe (mixing solutions of b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g with a volume ratio of 11:8), the insets are corresponding photos taken under a 365 nm UV-LED. (i) Fluorescence stability of the triple-emission probe when the probe colloid was successively exposed under a UV-LED.

head.ce:title: Acoustofluidics-manipulated triple-emission fluorescent nanoprobe aggregates with multicolor-variation for colorimetric quantitative assay

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acoustofluidics-assisted colorimetric method, a promising point-of-care test (POCT) strategy, has been applied in convenient detection of target molecules using the sensing nanomaterials, but remains a formidable challenge to design fluorescent probe with profuse color evolution, realizing sensitive, simple and accurate determination of analyte. Here, a novel triple-emission fluorescent probe is constructed through a simple blend of two ratiometric fluorescent probes (blue-organosilane-polymerized carbon dots@SiO2 nanoparticles@red-CdTe/CdS quantum dots (b@SiO2@r) and blue-organosilane-polymerized carbon dots@SiO2 nanoparticles@green-CdTe/CdS quantum dots (b@SiO2@g)) at the optimized volume ratio of 11:8. As signal reporters of the triple-emission probe, outer modified “r” and “g” will be quenched in sequence by target via two-step response strategy while the inner “b” as internal standard remains constant. When acoustic field is applied, the effect of acoustofluidics-based nanoparticles concentration makes triple-emission probe aggregate completely, accompanying fluorescence color signal being enhanced, sensitivity of colorimetric assay being improved and multicolor-variation (from orange to dark-goldenrod to dark-olive-green to sea-green to dark-cyan to final steel-blue) with the increase of analyte amount. For POCT demonstration, hemoglobin (Hb) is used as a model target and the captured fluorescence photos of triple-emission probe aggregates are analyzed by a chromaticity analysis application on the smartphone to calculate the red, green and blue values, which are used for accurate Hb quantification with the limit of detection of 1.99 mg/L. Whole test is finished within ∼12 min. The acoustofluidics-manipulated triple-emission probe biosensing strategy has also been applied to the rapid and visual quantitative detection of Hb in human blood.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials-based analytical methods have been widely used in the detection of target molecules in environmental and biological systems [1–3]. In particular, the colorimetric assay based on fluorescent nanomaterials has become increasingly popular due to easy carrying, instrument-free, convenient operation, real-time/on-site visual detection and intuitive signal readout [4,5]. As outstanding candidates of colorimetric nanoprobes, fluorescent semiconductor nanomaterials, such as carbon dots (CDs) or quantum dots (QDs), possess highly favorable properties including strong fluorescence, good biocompatibility, controllable surface modification and easy forming composites by integrating with other nanomaterials (e.g., SiO2 nanoparticles (SiO2 NPs)) [6–8]. To date, numerous colorimetric methods have been applied in the determination of tetracycline via B, N co-doped CDs [9], copper ions and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid with CDs [10], hydrogen peroxide and glucose using L-cysteine-mediated synthesis of QDs [11], circulating tumor cells by CdTe QDs [12], and so on. These studies were well established, but also suffered from some obvious insufficiencies, such as low sensitivity and few color evolution, restricting their practical applications [13–15]. Accordingly, for reliable, sensitive and accurate assay, it is still highly necessary to design ingenious fluorescent nanoprobe with multicolor-variation and integrate some compatible technologies and portable devices.

In order to improve the sensitivity of colorimetric assay, trapping and concentrating nanoprobes or objects from original sample solution is an effective way, because enrichment of a low concentration of nanoprobes could greatly improve the detection sensitivity [16,17]. Significantly, acoustofluidics, an emerging technology fusing acoustics and fluid mechanics, can effectively manipulate NPs to form aggregates by acoustic wave [18–20], and are characterized by versatility, contactless and nondestructive manipulation, precise control, excellent biocompatibility, no-washing-step as well as simple device fabrication [21–25]. Recently, various acoustofluidics-integrated platforms have been developed for concentrating target NPs, resulting in enhancement of fluorescence signal and improvement of detection sensitivity [25–29]. For example, Gracioso Martins et al. applied surface acoustic waves (SAW)-based mixing to a flow-injection assay system to strengthen the chemiluminescence signal for detection of L-proline [29]. Benefited from the SAW-based mixing, they realized a 100-fold enhancement of detection sensitivity. Nevertheless, these acoustofluidics-integrated platforms were almost constructed by single-emission fluorescent probe and only displayed fluorescence brightness changes by “turn on” mode with analytes, limiting the practical application of visual analysis.

For multicolor-variation-based fluorescent colorimetric analysis, the double-emission fluorescent probe has displayed great potential as ideal sensor, which include two phosphors, generally one functions as the signal report unit and another as the internal signal reference, producing color variations with the increase of analyte concentration [30,31]. Compared with single-emission probe, double-emission probe shows higher capability of visual analysis, but the corresponding color evolution window is still narrow, which only provides variation between two colors such as red–blue [32,33], green–blue [34,35] and red–green [36,37]. Diverse efforts have been devoted to develop some new strategies for the profuse color variation in visual assay. In previous study, we have demonstrated that the color evolution range of double-emission probe could be broadened by introducing one more emission probe, namely triple-emission fluorescent probe [38]. On the basis of above work, Yang and colleagues have further constructed a novel triple-emission fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymer probe for the visualization of target analyte [39]. Although distinct quenching rates between g-CdTe QDs and r-CdTe/ZnS QDs towards target molecules made more profuse color evolution than those of traditional triple-emission probe, ulteriorly improving the capability of visual analysis, the difference in quenching rates is not obvious enough. Thus, there is still a great need to fabricate newer triple-emission probe and develop novel response strategy (to expand the difference in quenching rates) for reliable multicolor-variation-based analysis of target with high sensitivity and accuracy to meet the requirement of practical application.

Taking above into consideration, we constructed a novel triple-emission fluorescent nanoprobe that was manipulated into aggregates by acoustofluidics with multicolor-variation for colorimetric assay. The triple-emission nanoprobe was composed of blue-organosilane-polymerized CDs@SiO2 NPs@red-CdTe/CdS QDs (b@SiO2@r) and blue-organosilane-polymerized CDs@SiO2 NPs@green-CdTe/CdS QDs (b@SiO2@g) at the volume ratio of 11:8. The preparation of b@SiO2@r or b@SiO2@g includes three steps: (1) embedding blue-organosilane-polymerized CDs (b-SiCDs) into SiO2 NPs, (2) electrostatically self-assembling poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA) on the surface of b@SiO2, and (3) coating red-CdTe/CdS QDs (r-QDs) or green-CdTe/CdS QDs (g-QDs) on PDDA layer (Scheme 1 a). For colorimetric measurement, r-QDs and g-QDs simultaneously act as the signal reporters while b-SiCDs is an internal standard. Meanwhile, acoustofluidics was used to aggregate the triple-emission nanoprobe in the center of glass tubing microchannel and enhance the local fluorescence color signals. The fluorescence photos of nanoprobe aggregates were captured by a smartphone loaded on the 3D-printed holder. Further, the red, green and blue (RGB) values of fluorescence photos could be collected by a chromaticity analysis application (APP) installed on the smartphone and be utilized for the quantification of model target hemoglobin (Hb) (Scheme 1b). The colorimetric method was also applied to the detection of Hb in human blood samples, displaying a potential to point-of-care testing (POCT) in rapid and accurate clinical diagnosis.

The morphological structures of b@SiO2, b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g were investigated by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a, spherical b@SiO2 have good dispersibility and uniform size, with their surface being smooth. After coating r-QDs or g-QDs, the b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g both have the similar good dispersibility and morphology to b@SiO2, and many dark dots were appreciably observed on the surface of b@SiO2, indicating the successful formation of the b@SiO2@QDs hybrid nanospheres. Meanwhile, the synthesis process of b@SiO2@QDs was verified by dynamic light scattering and zeta potentials. After each modification, the hydrodynamic diameters (HDD) of nanomaterials are slightly increased (Fig. 2b), suggesting the thin PDDA layer and small QDs have little influence on the diameter and dispersity of the b@SiO2. Additionally, the zeta potential of b@SiO2-PDDA increases from −52.2 mV to 31.7 mV after cationic PDDA assembly. Following the functionalization of anionic r-QDs and g-QDs, the zeta-potential decrease and saturate at a value of −39.9 mV and −39.8 mV, respectively, indicating an excellent colloidal stability (Fig. 2c). In a word, b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g have nearly the same morphological structures, particle sizes and surface charges. Thus, when b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g coexist in the glass tubing of developed acoustofluidic device, they will experience almost identical aggregation effect caused by the acoustic radiation force. Further detail of the demonstration of acoustofluidics-assisted NPs aggregation will be provided below.

Because of the similar elemental composition and functional groups of r-QDs and g-QDs, b@SiO2@r were selected as a demonstration module. The elemental composition of b@SiO2@r was characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The FT-IR spectra of b-SiCDs, r-QDs, b@SiO2 and b@SiO2@r are shown in Fig. 2d. The b@SiO2@r display the bend vibration peak, symmetrical stretching vibration peak and antisymmetrical stretching vibration peak of Si−O−Si groups at ∼463, ∼797 and ∼1088 cm−1, respectively, which are coincident with the typical peaks of pure SiO2 NPs [46]. Besides, the b@SiO2@r also exhibit slight absorption bands at ∼1403/∼1558 cm−1 (COO−), ∼1572/∼1640 cm−1 (O=C−NR) and ∼2930 cm−1(C−H), which are characteristic bands of r-QDs or b-SiCDs, reflecting the existence of r-QDs and b-SiCDs on or in the SiO2 NPs. The XPS survey spectrum of b@SiO2 is displayed in Fig. S5a. Six typical elementary peaks located at binding energies of 26, 104, 155, 285, 402 and 533 eV represent O(2s), Si(2p), Si(2s), C(1s), N(1s) and O(1s), respectively, confirming the presence of C, N, O and Si elements. Fig. 2e (left) shows that b@SiO2@r contain not only the same C, O and Si elements as b@SiO2 but also Te (572 eV and 583 eV), Cd (405 eV and 412 eV) and S (162 eV) elements, confirming the presence of r-QDs. The corresponding high-resolution spectra were shown in Fig. S5b-e and Fig. 2e (right), respectively. However, the peak corresponding to the N element is hardly observed for the synthesized b@SiO2@r, which can be attributed to the trace amounts of N element and possible overlap between the peaks at ∼402 eV of N(1s) and ∼405 eV of Cd(3d5/2). Meanwhile, the main elemental components of b@SiO2 and b@SiO2@r were also investigated by EDS spectra. For b@SiO2, there are C, N, O and Si elements with semi-quantitative atomic ratios of 15.91, 0.29, 55.53 and 28.28 at.%, respectively, obtained by integrating their corresponding EDS peak area. For b@SiO2@r, there are C, N, O, Si, Te, Cd and S elements with semi-quantitative atomic ratios of 10.86, 0.39, 57.75, 29.98, 0.14, 0.41 and 0.48 at.%, respectively (Fig. 2f). The corresponding EDS elemental mappings exhibit that the C, N, O, Si, Te, Cd and/or S elements were evenly distributed in/on the SiO2 NPs (Fig. S6 and Fig. 2g). The above results suggest the successful synthesis of b@SiO2@r.

The optical properties of b@SiO2@QDs were studied in detail. After being adhered on b@SiO2, the emission bands of r-QDs and g-QDs slightly shift from 630 and 540 nm to 639 and 543 nm, respectively (Fig. S7). In Fig. 2h, individual b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g show strong fluorescence emission peaks at ∼460/∼639 nm and ∼460/∼543 nm, respectively. Once mixing two probes to form the triple-emission probe, it presents three emission peaks which consist with individual b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g under the same excitation of 365 nm and emits orange fluorescence (inset of Fig. 2h). Fig. 2i shows that the fluorescence intensity ratio ((I639 + I543)/I460) of triple-emission probe almost keep constant after illumination for 2 h under a UV lamp, indicating its excellent fluorescence stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of acoustofluidic device and demonstration of fluorescence signal enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, acoustofluidic technology exerts an effect of concentrating the triple-emission probe, enhancing fluorescence color signals and further improving the detection sensitivity in a timesaving and contactless way. The manipulation of triple-emission probe was achieved in the acoustofluidic device (Fig. 3 ). The device is constituted of four components, that is, a square-miniature hollow glass tubing, a piezoelectric ceramic transducer (PZT), two glass sheets and a glass slide. The glass tubing with a 50 mm length, 200 μm internal diameter and 400 μm external diameter was chosen because its internal diameter roughly corresponds to the half wavelength (λ/2, ∼201 μm) of acoustic wave (λ/2 = cf /2f, where cf is the constant acoustic velocity in the fluid loaded in the glass tubing (∼1495 m/s) [28,47] and f is the applied frequency in this work (∼3.710 MHz)), ensuring the pressure node line generation along the center of the glass tubing microchannel and improving the concentration effect. The glass tubing was glued onto the PZT via a UV curing adhesive layer, which should be as thin and homogeneous as possible to ensure the high vibration transmission efficiency from PZT to the glass tubing. An input and an output silicone hose were separately connected at both ends of the glass tubing, which introduce probe colloid, sample solution or wash buffer. PZT, made up of lead zirconate titanate, can convert electrical energy into mechanical energy. In this work, the PZT with size of 20 × 15 × 2.1 mm was utilized to create a standing bulk acoustic waves (BAWs) field in the glass tubing microchannel and form a single pressure node line in the center of the glass tubing microchannel with a z-direction λ/2 resonance mode when the acoustic wave frequency was around 3.710 MHz. The glass tubing-adhered PZT was bonded onto a glass slide (75 × 25 × 2 mm) with two small glass sheets (4 × 10 × 2 mm/individual) providing a gap between the PZT and glass slide. The sheets can decrease the acoustic energy loss from the PZT into the glass slide and increase the concentration efficiency.

When a radio signal with a specific driving frequency is applied to the PZT through a function generator, a standing BAWs will be generated, travel along the z-direction and propagate into the fluid confined inside the glass tubing microchannel via the UV curing adhesive layer. Then, a strong standing BAWs field with pressure nodes (minimum pressure regions) will be formed arising from the reflection and confinement of the acoustic waves at the glass tubing wall. Meanwhile, the acoustic radiation force will be also generated due to the scattering of the standing BAWs in the glass tubing, which can push the NPs suspended in the sample fluid toward pressure nodes in the standing BAWs field, resulting in aggregation of NPs (Fig. 4 a). When the acoustics is OFF, target NPs is uniformly distributed in the glass tubing microchannel. After applying acoustic waves, a fusiform particles cluster is observed at the center of glass tubing microchannel. The dynamic process of acoustofluidics-assisted NPs aggregation within 30 s is provided in Movie S1 of Supporting Information. Because of the concentration of NPs, their fluorescence color signals were enhanced significantly under excited by a 365 nm ultraviolet light emitting diode (UV-LED) compared with the randomly distributed signals before concentration (Fig. 4b). As shown in Fig. S8, when the PZT was actuated by radio signal with a driving frequency of ∼3.710 MHz for 1 min, the various fluorescent NPs (b@SiO2, b@SiO2@r, b@SiO2@g as well as the mixture of b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g, namely, triple-emission fluorescent probe) were aggregated in the viewing area with strong different fluorescence colors (bright blue, red, green and orange), respectively. Therefore, the acoustofluidics-integrated platform can be used for the sensitive visual assay of targe analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Control of critical factors for enriching color variation
ce:section.ce:para: Hb, a tetrameric metalloprotein composed of a protein part (globin) and four iron-containing parts (heme) [48], is used as a model target for proof of concept in this work. Owing to the electrostatic interaction between −COOH groups of QDs and iron ion, a photoinduced electron transfer process would occur, where QDs functions as a donor and iron ion as an electron acceptor [49,50], leading to the fluorescence quenching of QDs. So, the assay principle of Hb is based on the fluorescence quenching of the triple-emission probe by iron ion. Notably, the diameter of the Hb molecule (∼6.4 nm) [51] is too small to be directly concentrated by the as-constructed acoustofluidics-based particles manipulation device. Thus, this method utilizes the electrostatic interaction between QDs of b@SiO2@r or b@SiO2@g and iron ions of Hb to adsorb Hb, and then concentrate the Hb-carried triple-emission probe.

To realize multicolor-variation-based colorimetric analysis of Hb, three critical factors, including mass ratio of PDDA to b@SiO2, adding order of b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g, and volume ratio of b@SiO2@r to b@SiO2@g, were optimized in details.

During the synthesis of b@SiO2@QDs, the PDDA functions as a bridge between QDs and b@SiO2, and the adding amount of PDDA decides the adsorbing amount of QDs and influences subsequent aggregation performance of b@SiO2@QDs. As represented in Fig. S9, the HDD of b@SiO2@r increases with the increment of mass ratio of PDDA to b-SiCDs@SiO2, accompanying the occurrence of b@SiO2@r pre-aggregation (Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. S11, when the mass ratio of PDDA to b@SiO2 is lower than 8:1, the fluorescence colors of concentration spots are not bright enough due to the low adsorbing amount of r-QDs, which go against the subsequent colorimetric analysis. Further, when the mass ratio of PDDA to b@SiO2 is higher than 8:1, b@SiO2@r form pre-aggregates (with poor dispersibility) and are difficult to be concentrated at the center of glass tubing microchannel by the acoustic radiation force, which is unfavorable for the acoustofluidics-assisted colorimetric assay. Based on the above experimental results, the optimized mass ratio of PDDA to b-SiCDs@SiO2 was set to be 8:1 for the following studies.

On the basis of the simple blend method of b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g, an incessant failure still occurred in achieving a multicolor-variation when the triple-emission probe was used to detect Hb. When b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g were mixed to interact with Hb together, the fluorescence intensities of r-QDs and g-QDs were simultaneously decreased with the increase of Hb concentration (Fig. 5 a(1)). In the one-step response strategy, little difference in the change rates of fluorescence intensity ratios (I639/I460 and I543/I460) is observed (Fig. 5b(1)), and the triple emission probe system only produces an orange–blue color evolution (inset of Fig. 5a(1)). Meanwhile, when r-CdTe QDs replaced r-QDs (r-CdTe/CdS QDs) in above probe system to provide red fluorescence, the sensitivity of b@SiO2@r-CdTe QDs (the detailed preparation procedures are shown in Supporting Information) to Hb is improved due to the lack of CdS shell protection (Fig. 5a(2)). The partly difference in change rates of fluorescence intensity ratios (Fig. 5b(2)) makes the fluorescence colors of the triple-emission probe vary from orange to dark-goldenrod to olive to blue (inset of Fig. 5a(2)). Although the color evolutions are richer, they are not obvious enough, especially the lack of “green color” evolution. Excitingly, the significant difference in change rates of fluorescence intensity ratios is observed (Fig. 5b(3)) when r-QDs and g-QDs were interacted with Hb in sequence by controlling the adding order of b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g (Scheme 1b). At low concentration of Hb, the fluorescence of r-QDs was drastically quenched but unchanged for g-QDs, whereas at high concentration of Hb, the fluorescence of r-QDs completely quenched and g-QDs displayed further quenching effect (Fig. 5a(3)). By the two-step response strategy, the triple-emission probe could provide a profuse fluorescence color variations from orange to dark-goldenrod to dark-olive-green to sea-green to dark-cyan to final steel-blue (the inset of Fig. 5a(3)). Moreover, the strategy is not time-consuming and the whole detection test can be completed in ∼12 min, only 5 min more than that of one-step response strategy (Fig. S12). Thus, the two-step response strategy was used in further experiments.

To further improve the visualization effect, the volume ratio of b@SiO2@r to b@SiO2@g were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S13, the triple-emission probe with 11:8 volume ratio of b@SiO2@r to b@SiO2@g displays the widest color variation, from orange to dark-goldenrod to dark-olive-green to sea-green to dark-cyan to final steel-blue (Fig. S13c). In addition, the advantage of the triple-emission probe for colorimetric analysis of Hb was also validated by comparison with the double-emission probe (Fig. 6 ). As shown in Fig. 6b-d, the double-emission probe only produces red–blue (for the 11:0 volume ratio of b@SiO2@r to b@SiO2@g), green–blue (for the 0:8 volume ratio of b@SiO2@r to b@SiO2@g) and bright orange–dark green (for the 11:8 volume ratio of SiO2@r to SiO2@g) color evolutions, which are inferior to those of the triple-emission probe (Fig. 6a). That is to say, the triple-emission probe possesses a more profuse color evolution than those of double-emission probes for visual determination of Hb. Hence, the triple-emission probe with 11:8 volume ratio of b@SiO2@r to b@SiO2@g was used in future experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent and colorimetric assay of Hb
ce:section.ce:para: Before the sensitivity assay, b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g were independently prepared in quintuplicate to verify the reproducibility of triple-emission probe. As shown in Fig. S14a, the signals (fluorescence, RGB value and color) of five batches of triple-emission probe (mixture of b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g in the absence of Hb) were almost same, indicating a good reproducibility of triple-emission probe. Moreover, b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g were separately stored in a 4 ℃ refrigerator and kept stable in 15 days (Fig. S14b). Subsequently, the response time of the triple-emission probe to Hb was studied. As shown in Fig. S15, the dynamic experiment demonstrates that the fluorescence response to 150 mg/L Hb is completed in ∼5 min. Thus, 5 min was set for the triple-emission probe interact with Hb in order to obtain better reproducibility and accuracy.

Under the optimal conditions, using SiO2 NPs as the fluorescent materials carrier, b-SiCDs as the internal standard and QDs as the recognition unit, a dual-mode sensing platform with fluorometry and colorimetry was constructed to detect Hb. First, fluorescence measurement was carried out. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, in the concentration ranging from 0 to 105 mg/L, the fluorescence of r-QDs is drastically decreased while fluorescence of g-QDs and b-SiCDs both keep stable, and in the concentration ranging from 105 to 165 mg/L, fluorescence of g-QDs is rapidly quenched while fluorescence of both r-QDs (quenched completely) and b-SiCDs remain constant. The fluorescence intensity ratio ((I639 + I543)/I460) declines proportionately and displays a good linear relationship with the concentration of Hb ranging from 15 to 165 mg/L (R2 = 0.9960) (Fig. 7b). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.67 mg/L based on the 3σ rule.

Simultaneously, a fluorescent colorimetric assay was also performed. For improving sensitivity and realizing real-time/on-site colorimetric detection, an integrative and portable visualization platform was constructed, which is composed of a 365 nm UV-LED with a bandpass filter to produce excitation light, an acoustofluidic device to concentrate NPs, a smartphone with an external 200 × magnifying lens (a 20 × mobile lens and a 10 × microscope objective) to capture fluorescence photos of clustered NPs in the acoustofluidic device and a 3D printed holder to provide a dark background (Fig. 7c-d and Fig. S16). The holder was designed through the CAD 2019 software and printed by a 3D printer. As shown in Fig. S17, the 3D printed holder consists of a smartphone holder (with several holes) and a device holder (with several slots) for precisely placing the magnifying lens-coated smartphone, UV-LED, bandpass filter and acoustofluidic device, whose positions are fixed by calculation in consideration of their shapes, sizes and working distances to ensure the successful operation of visual analysis. The inner surface of 3D printed holder was further sprayed with a coat of matt black paint to avoid the reflection of UV light. As shown in Scheme 1b, after mixing and interacting b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g with Hb in sequence, the mixing solution was injected into the glass capillary and applied a driving frequency of 3.710 MHz. One minute later, the smartphone could capture a series of fusiform concentration spots with bright fluorescence colors, which changed from orange to dark-goldenrod to dark-olive-green to sea-green to dark-cyan to final steel-blue with the increase of Hb concentration (from 0 to 165 mg/L) (Fig. 7e). To achieve an accurate colorimetric analysis, RGB values of the fluorescent concentration spots captured by smartphone were calculated by a chromaticity analysis APP installed on the smartphone, and the RGB values were used as indicators for Hb quantification because they have close relationship with Hb concentration. As shown in Fig. S18, the values of (R + G)/B exhibit a linear relationship with Hb concentration in the range of 15–165 mg/L, while the coefficients of determination (R2) of fitting curve were only 0.9619. To improve R2 value, a staged function composed of two proximate linear was fitted. As shown in Fig. 7f, the values of R/(G + B) versus the Hb concentration ranging from 15 to 105 mg/L can be fitted into a linear equation: R/(G + B) = −0.00714C Hb + 1.034, R2 = 0.9979, and the LOD calculated from 3σ rule was about 1.99 mg/L, which was 4–5 orders of magnitude lower than the minimum requirement of the normal human blood [52]. When the Hb concentration was higher than 105 mg/L, the ratio of G to B was also proportional to the Hb concentration (G/B = −0.0184C Hb + 3.853, R2 = 0.9906). These results reveal that the acoustofluidics-based colorimetric method possesses a great potential for sensitive and accurate detection of Hb.

The performance of the fluorescent/colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform is compared with those of previous works, and the results are listed in Table S1. The LOD of the proposed method is significantly higher than that of colorimetry [53,54]. Meanwhile, this method has a similar LOD to fluorometry [55,56] and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [57,58]. Although electrochemistry (EC) and/or photoelectrochemistry (PEC) [59,60] have higher sensitivity than this work, they require complex instrument as well as highly trained operator, and are not fit for the on-site assay. Moreover, The LOD of the proposed approach can meet the needs of practical detection, which are 5–6 and 4–5 orders of magnitude lower than the minimum requirement of the normal human blood [52] for fluorometry and colorimetry, respectively. Particularly, most of the as-mentioned methods for quantitative detection rely on single-mode readout, which can experience the defect of poor anti-interference ability. On the contrary, the dual-mode sensing platform can address the defect because it could produce two kinds of signals (fluorescence signal and color signal). The two different kinds of signals with independent transmission paths could mutually support to eliminate artificial errors, systematic errors and external factors as well as improve the accuracy. Compared to the other fluorescent/colorimetric dual-mode sensing method [61], the as-proposed method displays a more profuse color evolution (Table 1 ).

To assess the selectivity of the as-proposed method, the response of triple-emission probe to diverse possible interference substances including metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+ and Ca2+), small organic molecules (glucose (Glu), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), glutathione (GSH), glutamic acid (GA) and glycine (Gly)) and biomacromolecules (bovine serum albumin (BSA), ovalbumin (OVA) and human serum albumin (HSA)) were investigated, respectively. As shown in Fig. S19, all interference substances do not cause any change of signal responses (fluorescence, RGB value or color), which are identical to the blank probe. However, the extremely obvious changes are observed by the addition of Hb, which reflects an excellent specificity of the triple-emission probe to Hb.

To verify the availability and feasibility of the sensing platform for the detection of Hb in practical application, determination of Hb in human whole blood samples (collected from two healthy volunteers and two patients with anemia) was demonstrated here using the triple-emission probe. Generally, the minimum thresholds of Hb in human blood are about 120 g/L (specifically, 130 g/L for male, 120 g/L for non-pregnant female, 110 g/L for pregnant female and children younger than 5 years, as well as 115 g/L for children aged between 5 and 11.9 [52]). Hb amount below these minimum thresholds causes anemia, which can be connected with various clinical diseases, such as blood loss, nutritional deficiencies, genetic disorders, cancers, etc. [62,63]. Prior to the measurement, the samples were diluted 1000-times with ultrapure water to make the level of Hb correspond to linear range and reduce possible interferences. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the color of triple-emission probe changes from sea-green to dark-cyan to final steel-blue when the Hb level is higher than 120 mg/L, revealing the Hb level is normal and can be utilized for human health monitoring. For real colorimetric detection (Fig. 8b), the fluorescence colors at 107/104 and 74.4/73.6 mg/L Hb (obtained by fluorometry/colorimetry) in two anemia patient blood samples were accordant with the corresponding colors in Fig. 7e, respectively, indicating a discernible dosage capability by the human eye. Additionally, similar results were also obtained in two normal human blood samples, but each Hb level was higher than that of anemia patient. The above as-obtained results are almost consistent with those from sodium lauryl sulfate-hemoglobin (SLS-Hb) method [64]. Thus, the sensing method can offer a clear and fast judgment whether the human Hb is at a normal or abnormal level for the rapid diagnosis of anemia. Meanwhile, when the known amount of Hb was added into human blood samples, the recovery of Hb ranged from 96.0 to 106% with the relative standard deviations (RSD) lower than 5.00%, which prove that the sensing platform has an excellent availability and feasibility to satisfy the demand of the clinical application.

In summary, a novel triple-emission fluorescent probe was successfully fabricated by mixing b@SiO2@r and b@SiO2@g at the optimized volume ratio of 11:8 for multicolor-variation-based colorimetric assay of Hb. The successful key factors of this work mainly include the following several points: (1) b-SiCDs covalently bind with the silicon element in SiO2 NPs to exhibit strong internal standard robustness, and QDs can rapidly adsorb on the surface of b@SiO2 via electrostatic interactions with the help of PDDA; (2) acoustofluidics can manipulate triple-emission nanoprobe to form aggregates, enhancing local fluorescence color signals and visual performance; (3) the two-step response strategy makes triple-emission probe display a profuse color evolution (from orange to dark-goldenrod to dark-olive-green to sea-green to dark-cyan to final steel-blue) after addition of target Hb; and (4) the RGB analysis has an excellent quantitation ability to target Hb. We have developed an acoustofluidics-assisted colorimetric method by concentrating triple-emission nanoprobe to accomplish the sensitive detection of Hb in human blood samples, which greatly exceeds our previously reported results [24] (e.g., time-consuming preparation processes of fluorescent probe, narrow color variation and single-mode readout). The as-proposed method possesses many merits, such as superior specificity, inherent self-correction of fluorescence, contactless operation and portable feasibility (no costly instrument used). Especially, this method can give a distinct judgement whether or not persons are anemia.

Generally, the fluorescent sensing principle is the direct/indirect interaction between fluorescent nanomaterial and target, resulting in the “turn on” or “turn off” of fluorescence signal. Therefore, for the specific structural characteristics of target molecules, we will fabricate various triple-emission probes using different functionalized semiconductor nanomaterials so that the acoustofluidics-assisted colorimetric method can be further expanded into the on-site visual assay of other target molecules (e.g., pesticides, heavy metals, persistent organic pollutants, endogenous active substances, tumor markers and so on) in more specific areas (such as environmental monitoring, safety assessment and clinical diagnosis).
